title: Cisco IOS Basic Configuration
description: Learn standard commands for navigating IOS modes, configuring device hostnames, setting up interfaces, and saving configurations.

tasks.explanation: The 'enable' command transitions you from user EXEC mode (Router>) to privileged EXEC mode (Router#), unlocking diagnostic and configuration options.

tasks.hint: Use the command: 'configure terminal' or 'conf t'
tasks.explanation: Entering 'configure terminal' allows you to make system-wide changes. The prompt changes to Router(config)#.
The 'hostname' command modifies the identity of the device, updating the prompt representation instantly.

tasks.prompt: Enter Interface Configuration mode for interface 'GigabitEthernet0/1'.
tasks.hint: Use interface command followed by the interface name: 'interface GigabitEthernet0/1' or 'int g0/1'
tasks.explanation: To configure a specific port, enter Interface Configuration mode. The prompt changes to Core-Switch(config-if)#.

tasks.prompt: Configure the interface IP address to '192.168.1.1' with a subnet mask of '255.255.255.0'.
tasks.explanation: The 'ip address' command assigns the IPv4 settings to the selected logical or physical interface.

tasks.prompt: Enable/activate the interface (bring it administratively up).
tasks.hint: Use the command: 'no shutdown' or 'no shut'
tasks.explanation: Cisco interfaces are disabled (shutdown) by default. The 'no shutdown' command turns the interface on.

tasks.prompt: Exit the interface configuration mode back to global configuration mode.
tasks.explanation: The 'exit' command takes you up one level in the configuration hierarchy.

tasks.prompt: Exit global configuration mode back to privileged EXEC mode.
tasks.explanation: Using 'exit' or 'end' moves you out of config mode back to privileged EXEC mode.

tasks.prompt: Save the current running configuration to the startup configuration so it persists across reboots.
tasks.hint: Use the copy command or write shortcut: 'copy running-config startup-config' or 'write'
tasks.explanation: The active configuration is stored in volatile RAM. Saving it to NVRAM using copy run start or write ensures it survives a reboot.
